step_1_context.text: We can use either will or be going to to talk about something that is planned, or something that we think is likely to happen in the future:

step_2_explanation.simple_rule:
We can use either will or be going to to talk about something that is planned, or something that we think is likely to happen in the future:
 • We will study climate change in a later part of the course.
step_2_explanation.formula:
(or We are going to study …)
 • Where will you stay in Berlin?
step_2_explanation.common_mistake:
We use will rather than be going to to make a prediction based on our opinion or experience:
 • Why not come over at the weekend?

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 9.1.1 Have you seen Nadia recently? She'll have another baby.
options: Should be 'is going to have' (present evidence)
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: Should be 'is going to have' (present evidence)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Present evidence (she's visibly pregnant) → use 'be going to'.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 9.1.2 The method is quite simple, and I'm sure it will be familiar to most of you.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Prediction based on opinion with 'I'm sure' → will.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 9.1.3 B: I'll see you tomorrow evening, then.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Decision made at the moment of speaking → will.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 9.1.4 Are these new skis yours? Will you take up skiing?
options: Should be 'Are you going to take up' (prior plan)
step_3_interactive_exercises.correct_answer: Should be 'Are you going to take up' (prior plan)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Asking about a previously decided plan → be going to.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 9.1.5 Wherever you go in Brazil, you'll find the people very friendly.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: General prediction based on experience → will.
